title: SaMoE: Parameter Efficient MoE Language Models via Self-Adaptive Expert Combination

structured.summary: The paper proposes SaMoE, a parameter-efficient Mixture-of-Experts architecture that shares a global expert pool across layers via learned soft linear combinations and incorporates adaptive shortcut connections to improve gradient flow. SaMoE achieves up to 5.2× parameter reduction compared to standard MoE models while maintaining or improving pretraining perplexity and zero-shot task accuracy on billion-scale autoregressive language models.

strengths.point: Novel parameter-efficient MoE design using soft adaptive expert sharing
strengths.evidence: SaMoE decouples expert parameters from model depth by learning linear combinations of a shared expert pool, enabling significant parameter reduction without accuracy loss (Section 3, 4.3).

strengths.point: Improved gradient flow via self-adaptive shortcut connections
strengths.evidence: Learned residual scaling parameters per MoE layer help stabilize training and improve expert utilization, as validated in ablations (Section 3.2, 4.5).

strengths.point: Comprehensive empirical evaluation on billion-scale AR language models
strengths.evidence: SaMoE outperforms baselines including AR-MoE, PR-MoE, and Grouped-MoE in pretraining perplexity and zero-shot accuracy while using fewer parameters (Section 4.3, Tables 1 & 2).

strengths.point: Ablation studies demonstrate the necessity of proposed components
strengths.evidence: Ablations confirm that both soft expert sharing and shortcut connections contribute to performance gains, outperforming naïve or heuristic parameter sharing (Section 4.5).

weaknesses.point: Lack of comparison to other recent parameter-efficient MoE methods
weaknesses.evidence: The paper omits empirical or discussion-based evaluation against approaches like Switch Transformers with shared experts or low-rank/factorized expert methods, limiting contextualization of novelty (Critique #1).

weaknesses.point: Insufficient justification and analysis of soft linear combination design
weaknesses.evidence: No ablations or theoretical justification are provided for choosing learned linear combinations over other mixing functions such as gating or nonlinear attention (Critique #2).

weaknesses.point: Sparse details on training hyperparameters and stability techniques
weaknesses.evidence: Initialization schemes, learning rates, normalization or constraints on expert coefficients and shortcut scalars are not described, hindering reproducibility (Critique #3, #11).

weaknesses.point: Limited evaluation scope restricted to autoregressive language models
weaknesses.evidence: Generalization to other model architectures (encoder-only, encoder-decoder) or modalities is not explored (Critique #4).

weaknesses.point: No analysis of expert pool size impact on overfitting or expert diversity
weaknesses.evidence: Potential redundancy or specialization bottlenecks from sharing a global expert pool are not discussed or empirically analyzed (Critique #5, #12).

weaknesses.point: Missing runtime, communication overhead, and efficiency metrics
weaknesses.evidence: Although SaMoE requires all-to-all communication, no measurements of throughput, latency, or training cost are reported to assess practical trade-offs (Critique #6, #10).

weaknesses.point: Baselines might not be fully parameter or training matched
weaknesses.evidence: It is unclear if baseline models have matched depth, width, or training schedules, possibly biasing comparisons (Critique #7).
Expert coefficient normalization or constraints are vaguely described, making it difficult to interpret the combination mechanism precisely (Critique #13).

weaknesses.point: Limited discussion on open-source release details
weaknesses.evidence: The timeline and scope of code and model release remain unspecified, reducing community impact expectations (Critique #14).

weaknesses.point: Uncertainty about scalability to very large (10B+) models
weaknesses.evidence: Experiments focus on models up to ~5.8B parameters, leaving open whether gains persist at larger scales (Critique #15).

questions.question: How does SaMoE perform on encoder-only or encoder-decoder architectures and other modalities?
questions.motivation: Understanding generality beyond autoregressive language modeling is critical for broader applicability.

questions.question: What are the practical training and inference costs (speed, memory, communication overhead) of SaMoE compared to standard MoE?
questions.motivation: Parameter efficiency gains are valuable only if runtime and hardware efficiency are not adversely impacted.

questions.question: Can alternative expert combination mechanisms (e.g., gating, attention) improve performance or parameter efficiency compared to soft linear combinations?
questions.motivation: Exploring different combination functions may yield better gradient flow or capacity utilization.

questions.question: Does the shared expert pool limit layer specialization and model capacity due to expert redundancy or bottlenecks?
questions.motivation: Ensuring sufficient expert diversity is important for model expressiveness and transferability.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of comparison to other recent parameter-efficient MoE methods. The paper omits empirical or discussion-based evaluation against approaches like Switch Transformers with shared experts or low-rank/factorized expert methods, limiting contextualization of novelty (Critique #1)
critique_points.point_002: Insufficient justification and analysis of soft linear combination design. No ablations or theoretical justification are provided for choosing learned linear combinations over other mixing functions such as gating or nonlinear attention (Critique #2)
critique_points.point_003: Sparse details on training hyperparameters and stability techniques. Initialization schemes, learning rates, normalization or constraints on expert coefficients and shortcut scalars are not described, hindering reproducibility (Critique #3, #11)
critique_points.point_004: Limited evaluation scope restricted to autoregressive language models. Generalization to other model architectures (encoder-only, encoder-decoder) or modalities is not explored (Critique #4)
critique_points.point_005: No analysis of expert pool size impact on overfitting or expert diversity. Potential redundancy or specialization bottlenecks from sharing a global expert pool are not discussed or empirically analyzed (Critique #5, #12)
critique_points.point_006: Missing runtime, communication overhead, and efficiency metrics. Although SaMoE requires all-to-all communication, no measurements of throughput, latency, or training cost are reported to assess practical trade-offs (Critique #6, #10)
critique_points.point_007: Baselines might not be fully parameter or training matched. It is unclear if baseline models have matched depth, width, or training schedules, possibly biasing comparisons (Critique #7)
critique_points.point_008: Mathematical notation clarity issues. Expert coefficient normalization or constraints are vaguely described, making it difficult to interpret the combination mechanism precisely (Critique #13)
critique_points.point_009: Limited discussion on open-source release details. The timeline and scope of code and model release remain unspecified, reducing community impact expectations (Critique #14)
critique_points.point_010: Uncertainty about scalability to very large (10B+) models. Experiments focus on models up to ~5.8B parameters, leaving open whether gains persist at larger scales (Critique #15)
